utterances: Thank you.
We're talking with Julian Barnes, the Pentagon reporter for The Wall Street Journal.
Also with us, Steve Vladeck, law professor at American University's Washington College of Law.
Drones and the Brennan confirmation hearings.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION, from NPR News.

And, Julian, back to these targeted killings in just a moment, but there will be other subjects to come up at Mr. Brennan's confirmation hearings, among them his - he was a longtime Central Intelligence Agency officer, and in the Bush administration, participated in some meetings where - again, we're back to euphemisms - enhanced interrogation, i.e. waterboarding, or some would have it torture, was condoned.
That's right.
That's what derailed John Brennan as the nominee to head the CIA during Mr. Obama's first term, questions about whether he didn't speak up against this enough.
He says that he personally opposed it, and wasn't directly involved.

But senators have said that they want to get more answers from him on exactly what his involvement was during the Bush administration for the approval and use of these, as you said, waterboarding and other enhanced interrogation techniques.

And there will be some Republicans certainly who haven't gotten some answers from Secretary Panetta today on Benghazi, will be asking Mr. Brennan what the White House knew about what was going on there.
That's right.
Benghazi has not gone away.
We saw some pretty serious questioning of General Martin Dempsey and Mr. Panetta this morning.
And John Brennan was as - was at least involved on the edges of this, and so we're going to see some questions about that.
Now, we don't expect him to illuminate a lot about Benghazi that we haven't already heard from Morrell, the acting director of the CIA, or Hillary Clinton.
But it's likely to come up.
All right.
Let's see if we can go to Nikki, and Nikki on the line with us from Nashville.
Hi.
How are you guys?
Good.
Thanks.
I know that drones have held a lot of controversy over the past couple of years, about taking the human element out of war.
It reduces casualties, some people say.

But, at the same time, it puts less people on the ground to work, you know, work with local communities, tribes that may not have access to, you know, big cities and stuff, where maybe the Taliban or al-Qaida or any places like that they're hiding, you know.
It's difficult to fight a transnational organization simply because there's so many people.
And I would like to see the international community focus more on pumping money into, you know, weaponry and the military and more on diplomacy and economic sanctions in those areas.
I think that's one of the best ways we can get communities on our side to not endorse the Taliban or al-Qaida, or to not turn to extreme measures to gain their livelihood back.
Because a lot of these people are very deprived and...
And I don't mean to cut you off, Nikki, but we're running out of time.

Steve Vladeck, I think probably a good case could be made that what Nikki's definition would fit Yemen, a place that is impoverished, a huge population, youth population, and where al-Qaida is active.

And I think that's a very question for this administration - and, really, for the administration that comes after it - about just what position we're going to take, vis-a-vis, Yemen, how much resources we're going to invest in Yemen from an infrastructure perspective, from a support perspective.
You know, part of the problem that led to the Awlaki attack is that we really don't have a sufficiently strong relationship with the Yemeni government, where we can say we have this guy.
We need you to help us get him.
Nor does the Yemeni government have a sufficiently strong infrastructure to control much outside of the capital city.
Well, that's exactly the problem, is that in Sana'a, there's actually a decent amount of control, but not in the north and not where - not in the parts of Yemen where these strikes were taking place.
You know, I mean, not to sort of tie two things together, but, Neal, this is also a part of why it's been so hard for the administration to close Guantanamo.
There are some 35 Yemeni detainees there who have been cleared for release and who can't be sent back because we just don't have a full handle on and don't trust the situation on the ground there.
So I think that's going to be a huge foreign policy priority and problem for this administration, and for several to come.
Nikki, thanks very much.
We'll let a couple of emailers get the last word.

citizen who joins with an organization against the U.S.
has effectively renounced their citizenship, no longer protected by the Constitution.
Who gets to decide that?
I'm an anti-war activist back in the '60s, but I do not object to a police force using deadly fire if necessary against armed criminals with intent to kill.
From NPR News, this is NEWS AND NOTES.
I'm Farai Chideya.
I'm sitting in for Ed Gordon.
You, too.
So, anyway, gentlemen, thank you very much for your time today.
We appreciate it.
Julian Barnes of The Wall Street Journal, and Steve Vladeck of the American University's Washington College of Law.
Penguins coming up next.
It's the TALK OF THE NATION.
This is News & Notes.
I'm Farai Chideya.
If someone you know has five scotches at a family party, does that mean he's an addict?
What if she goes out of the house and comes back glassy-eyed?
Sometimes addiction is easy to spot, like when you see someone you know and love nodding out on a street.
If this happens in your family, what do you do?
When and how should you step in?
Dr.
Henry Gregory directs the Rafiki Consortium, which gives public and private agencies training on dealing with mental health issues.
And a woman we'll call Charlotte has a brother who went through an intervention led by Dr. Gregory.
We've also got with us Michael Middleton, a recovering heroin addict.
I'm really glad all of you are here to share your stories.
Thank you.
Hello, Farai.
Hi.
Welcome.
Well, I'm going to start with you, Dr. Gregory.
You have been working in family therapy for over 30 years.
So, how would you describe your approach to families specifically when you deal with substance abuse counseling?
Well, I think it's very important to get the family involved.
There's definitely evidence that says that a family intervention is more successful than individual intervention.

So, it's all about relationships in the black community, and the more you can activate those relationships through a loving, respectful intervention, the better chance you have of getting the person in treatment, retaining them in treatment, and even minimizing relapse.
So, what does a loving, respectful intervention mean?

I mean, how do you - you're really talking about taking one group of people's world view, a family that says you should act in this certain way, hopefully a healthy way, and someone else's worldview, which says that I have to do this substance or action every day.
How do you make that a respectful interaction?
Well, you talk about how the addiction or the usage affects you, what your thoughts and feelings are.
See, abuse only thrives when there is a collusion of silence.
At some level, the abuser begins to take up all the emotional space in the family, and then the family is silenced verbally and emotionally.
For the intervention, you just activate those voices.

So, people begin to talk about how these things affect them watching the addict go through what he goes through, how it feels to have missed that person in an honest reciprocal relationship, and giving that feedback very frequently gets the addict's attention.
All right, Charlotte, I'm glad you're talking with us.
Now, why did your family approach Dr. Gregory for help with your brother?
My brother, who came out of Vietnam, went and came out of Vietnam, had long years of drinking, heavy drinking, and then also drug abuse.
Because he was living in the house with me and my mother, who was quite elderly, we went through a lot, the siphoning off of money, some bad attitude, some negative words, et cetera, et cetera.

And then my mother, not being able to see what was happening, and then my being able to see it, telling her about it, but her not really believing me, I said, Maria, you're - Charlotte, you're going to have to go and get somebody to help you with this.
So, I knew Dr. Gregory, and we talked, and his first concern is that we have got to get your mother onboard.
We have got to talk to your mother and see if she is willing to help us, or let us do what we need to do to intervene.
So, she agreed, we went out to lunch, talked, and they presented to her that we're going to have to literally get him out of the house.
And that's what we had to do.
It was - I didn't know what else to do, so that's why I turned to Dr. Gregory, and I knew his strategy would be something that was peaceful and not, you know, violent or totally traumatic.
So, that's the route we went.
Dr.
Gregory, back to you.
You've been involved in dozens of interventions.
How did this one play out?
Well, it was interesting.
What we agreed is that Charlotte and her mother would not be home when it happened.
So, we scheduled it, and one morning about eight folks just converged on the house where her brother was sleeping, knocked on the - his best friend knocked on the door.
And when he opened the door we all came in.
He looked around, he looked very surprised, and we went in the room and sat down and started talking.
And he had a very interesting reply to our intervention.
He said, what took you so long?
He's a kind of a - how should I say it?

A unique kind of guy, kind of sarcastic, but I thought - I think that captured, at some level, he wanted somebody to stop him, and he couldn't do it on his own, and he trusted the people who were involved.
Although they were not family members, in the black community family is extended family.
It includes biological and psychological members.
And these were people who were very connected to him, and he sat down and let them give him feedback and tell him what their concerns were.
And he basically submitted to the process.
Now, I'll say, that day he took a shower, got his clothes on, we took him directly to a treatment facility where he stayed for, I forget, a couple of months.
And since that time he continued in treatment and has basically emancipated from the family.
Even though he was an adult several times over biologically, he's basically emancipated.
He's on his own and doing well at this point.
Well, Michael, don't think I've forgotten about you, but I want to ask Charlotte one more question first.
Do you think this was a success, and why or why not?
I think it was a success for my brother in that it might have been the first time he actually turned around and stepped off of the drugs and the alcohol.
I am hoping he's still off the drugs and the alcohol.
He's not in the same state now.
It was extremely difficult for my mother.
Her health went downhill from there.
She's since passed.
But yes, successful - I think it's the best way and probably the only way we could have done it.
There is no way that she and I could have done it.
There's no way that we could have intervened.
We really had to have someone close to us to come in and do it.
So yes, it was successful.
I don't mind taking hits on my record on issues, but when somebody starts throwing mud, at least we can hope that it's both accurate and not right out of the Republican playbook, because what I...
My passion comes because of something that has happened.
But nobody ever talks about what happened before.
Nobody ever talks about that history and all that stuff that made me upset.
Why I have Mississippi tattooed on my back - it's for a reason, not a season.
Why do you have Mississippi tattooed on your back?
I carry some responsibility on my back.
No matter how many movies I do, no matter how much money I get, when I take off my shirt I will always be reminded of what I'm supposed to be doing.
Whether I do it or not is something that I will have to pay for, that I will have to stand or fall for.
It sounds like you're pretty hard on yourself.
Who knows?
But, if not, you know, if not, who?
Because if I'm light on myself, then am I going to do the things that I'm supposed to do?
You know, because everybody's light, and they're partying, and they're having fun, but folks are messed up.
Let's listen to a little bit of one of...
And we can lighten up, if you want to.
No, we will.
(Singing) Fightin' is a habit, put it into hell.
(Soundbite of laughter)
